summary: Gruber dissects Mark Zuckerberg's prepared talking points that were photographed during his congressional testimony, specifically the section addressing Tim Cook's criticism of Facebook's business model. The notes contained three bracketed points attempting to draw equivalence between Apple's and Facebook's data practices, which Zuckerberg never actually used during testimony. Gruber systematically dismantles each point, arguing that iOS requires explicit user permission for data access while Facebook has been devious about privacy. He notes that Apple's worst offense was naivety about third-party app data access, which they quickly corrected, while Facebook actively scraped call and SMS data from Android devices. Gruber concludes that any attempt to equate Apple and Facebook on privacy is laughable.
key_points: Zuckerberg's prepared notes contained talking points equating Apple's and Facebook's data practices, which he never actually delivered during testimony
iOS requires explicit user permission for accessing private data and makes such access easily revocable, unlike Facebook's approach
Apple's worst privacy failing was naivety about third-party app access to address books, which they quickly fixed; Facebook's problem is deliberate deviousness about privacy
Facebook collected phone call and SMS history from Android devices but couldn't do so on iOS because Apple never allowed that access
The Bezos 'charge you less' defense is specious because Facebook's free service extracts value through data collection, not generosity
money_quotes: There is almost nothing similar about how Apple and Facebook treat user data.
Installing an app on an iPhone is nothing like signing in to Facebook.

Apple was, at worst, naive about what apps would do with unfettered access to users' address books. Facebook is facing criticism today — and losing its users' trust — not because they've been naive about privacy, but because they've been devious about it.
But what a pile of horseshit they would have been if he had.
Trying to argue that Apple is in the same boat as Facebook on the privacy front is simply laughable.
Nobody is arguing that Apple collects too much data about users.
key_insight: Apple's privacy stance is fundamentally different from Facebook's because Apple was at worst naive about third-party data access while Facebook has been deliberately devious about exploiting user data.
